what_manipulated: interpersonal risk (threat to relationship security) and goal strength (connectedness vs self-protection priority)
what_measured: goal activation patterns and behavioral approach or avoidance toward relationship dependence
experiment: Units are individuals varying in self-esteem. Independent variables include presence of interpersonal risk cues and executive control capacity. Dependent measures capture implicit and explicit goal activation, assessed via reaction times and direct report. The design tests whether risk-triggered goal conflicts are resolved differently based on self-esteem level and executive capacity. Comparisons are between conditions differing in risk salience and stated goal strength. Analysis examines main effects and conditional patterns across self-esteem groups.

what_manipulated: presence of a self-handicap (excuse availability) and success or failure outcome
what_measured: self-esteem and subsequent task preparation and performance
experiment: Units are individuals assigned to conditions with or without self-handicap availability prior to task performance. Some units experience success, others failure. Outcome measures include post-failure self-esteem ratings and preparation time invested in a subsequent task, with final task performance as the terminal outcome. Analysis traces whether self-handicap presence leads to excuse-generation thoughts, which buffer self-esteem but reduce future motivation. Statistical comparisons examine direct and mediated effects across conditions.

what_manipulated: diversity cues in organizational settings (minority representation level and diversity philosophy framing)
what_measured: perceived identity-contingent threats and institutional trust
experiment: Units are individuals exposed to workplace setting vignettes or materials varying in minority representation (low vs implied higher) and stated diversity approach (colorblindness vs valuing diversity). Primary outcome is self-report of perceived threat tied to group identity. Secondary outcome is reported trust in the setting. Analysis includes direct effects of cues on threat and mediation through perceived identity-contingent evaluations. Comparisons are between factorial combinations of representation and philosophy cues.

what_manipulated: cognitive fluency (ease of processing stimuli) via perceptual, conceptual, or linguistic difficulty
what_measured: level of abstraction in mental construal of world events
experiment: Units are individuals exposed to stimuli or text varying in processing difficulty. Fluency is manipulated by altering visual clarity, conceptual priming availability, or linguistic simplicity. Construal is measured via categorization responses, distance ratings, or description abstractness. Analysis compares construal patterns between fluent and disfluent conditions. A secondary analysis tests whether perceived distance mediates the effect of fluency on abstraction. Statistical tests examine means and mediation pathways.

what_manipulated: presence of linguistic directionality cues (sentences specifying spatial or color-direction relations) during a memory delay
what_measured: accuracy of maintaining and retrieving feature-location associations
experiment: Units are young children who view a target image (color-location conjunction), experience a delay, then match it to an array. Manipulation provides linguistic labels specifying directional relationships or non-linguistic cues (flashing, pointing) during delay. Dependent variable is match accuracy, with errors coded as confusions with reflections or other patterns. Analysis compares error rates and match performance across linguistic, non-linguistic, and control conditions. A secondary analysis correlates individual differences in directional language knowledge with performance improvement.

what_manipulated: nonconscious goal activation via priming of achievement goal during learning of novel task structure
what_measured: speed and accuracy of learning implicit task patterns and structure
experiment: Units are individuals who either receive priming of achievement goals or neutral priming, then complete implicit learning tasks with novel, hidden structure. Dependent measure is learning rate, quantified by accuracy or error reduction across trials. Tasks involve novel environment or sequence patterns. Analysis compares learning trajectories and final accuracy between primed and control conditions. Statistical tests examine whether goal priming facilitates learning speed independent of conscious strategy or awareness.

what_manipulated: gender of observer (male vs female) interpreting nonverbal behavioral cues
what_measured: accuracy and bias in perceiving dating-relevant intent (sexual interest vs friendliness) in observed behavior
experiment: Units are individuals rating ambiguous nonverbal cues (facial expressions, proximity, contact) presented in dating contexts. Signal-detection analysis separates perceptual sensitivity (ability to discriminate true signals from noise) from decision bias (leniency threshold). Dependent measures include hit rate, false-alarm rate, and derived d-prime and criterion statistics. Analysis compares males and females on sensitivity and bias. Results indicate whether gender differences reflect lower detectability or different response criteria.

what_manipulated: precision of numerical anchor (precise specific value vs rounded approximate value) presented before numerical estimation
what_measured: magnitude of adjustment away from anchor in subsequent numerical judgment
experiment: Units are individuals who receive an anchor value (precise or rounded) then make numerical estimates of unknown quantities. Dependent variable is adjustment, quantified as the difference between estimate and anchor, normalized by the range of plausible values. Analysis compares adjustment magnitude between precise and rounded anchor conditions. Statistical tests examine whether precise anchors produce smaller overall adjustments. Secondary analyses test whether fine-resolution mental representation of precise anchors explains the effect.

what_manipulated: difficulty and conflict level of evaluative judgments (moral stance questions with opposing intuitions)
what_measured: trajectory and dynamics of reaching movements toward response options
experiment: Units are individuals making evaluative judgments (agree/disagree) while their hand movements toward response buttons are tracked. Dependent measures include trajectory curvature, peak velocity timing, and attraction toward non-chosen option during the reach. Difficult, conflicting judgments are compared against easier, consensus judgments. Analysis examines whether simultaneous motor activation toward both response alternatives is observable even when only one is ultimately selected, using continuous movement tracking.

what_manipulated: personal valuation of prediction accuracy (perceived value of being optimistic vs accurate) through questionnaire and belief elicitation
what_measured: endorsement of what prediction style (accurate, optimistic, pessimistic) is appropriate for oneself and others
experiment: Units are individuals asked to report normative beliefs about what kind of prediction they and others should make. Independent variable is implicit framing of prediction context to activate beliefs about optimism's value. Dependent measures are ratings or choices reflecting preference for accuracy versus optimistic bias. Analysis examines whether people endorse optimism as normatively appropriate, varying by target (self vs other). Statistical comparisons test prediction-style preferences across contexts.

what_manipulated: age group (younger adults vs older adults) as a quasi-experimental factor in memory and imagination task
what_measured: episodic specificity (internal detail richness) in recalled past events and imagined future events
experiment: Units are younger and older adults who generate descriptions of past and future personal events. Transcribed narratives are segmented and coded for episodic detail (sensory, spatial, temporal) versus semantic detail (facts, knowledge). Dependent variable is count of internal details per event. Analysis compares age groups on detail richness for past events, future events, and examines correlation between past and future detail production. A secondary analysis correlates individual differences in relational memory ability with detail generation.

what_manipulated: speaker's eye gaze direction (toward shared vs private objects) and utterance framing (assertive statement vs question)
what_measured: accuracy and use of speaker's gaze as cue to word referent
experiment: Units are preschool-aged children (2-4 years) who hear a novel word label in contexts where speaker visibility differs. In one condition, the speaker can see only one of two objects; in another, the speaker cannot see one object at all. Utterances either assert a label ("There's the...") or question ("Where's the..."). Dependent measure is which object the child selects. Analysis compares selection accuracy across age groups and utterance types, testing whether children use gaze as referential intent cue beyond simple attention-following. Secondary analysis examines pragmatic reasoning.

what_manipulated: number of independent estimates combined (crowd size and composition)
what_measured: accuracy of aggregated estimate versus individual estimate accuracy
experiment: Units are individuals or groups making numerical estimates of quantities. Dependent measure is estimation error, calculated as absolute deviation from true value. Analysis examines whether averaging multiple individual estimates reduces error compared to individual performance, and tests conditions under which crowd wisdom emerges. Comparisons examine relationship between individual error independence and crowd advantage. Statistical analysis includes error variance decomposition.

what_manipulated: stimulus onset asynchrony (timing between target presentation and subsequent mask) in rapid serial visual presentation
what_measured: temporal selection properties (suppression, delay, diffusion) of attentional filtering
experiment: Units are individuals viewing rapid sequences of stimuli with two targets separated by variable timing (stimulus onset asynchrony). Dependent measure is distractor intrusion rates analyzed as function of temporal position. Analysis decomposes attentional selection into efficacy (suppression), timing (latency of onset), and precision (temporal diffusion). Statistical examination of each parameter's time course shows differential recovery at different stimulus onset asynchronies. Comparisons reveal distinct underlying mechanisms.

what_manipulated: glucose availability (sugar-sweetened vs artificially-sweetened drink) and prior self-control depletion task
what_measured: reliance on heuristic-based decision making versus effortful rule-based analysis
experiment: Units are individuals who either consume a glucose-restoring or placebo drink, then perform a self-control task (or control condition), then make decisions with varying heuristic temptation (attraction effect). Dependent variable is attraction effect magnitude, quantified by shift in preference when a decoy option is added. Analysis tests whether glucose consumption interacts with self-control depletion to reduce heuristic reliance. Comparisons examine decision quality and choice patterns across conditions.

what_manipulated: commission of an error versus correct response during a task
what_measured: defensive startle response magnitude and error-related negativity amplitude
experiment: Units are individuals performing a task where errors are made and correct responses occur. Electrophysiological measures capture error-related negativity (ERP component at ~50ms post-error). Simultaneously, acoustic startle probes assess defensive startle magnitude. Dependent measures include ERP amplitude and startle response latency and magnitude. Analysis compares startle responses following errors versus correct trials. Secondary analysis examines whether individual differences in error-related negativity predict startle potentiation magnitude.

what_manipulated: fairness of economic offer (fair vs unfair distribution) while holding monetary payoff constant
what_measured: subjective happiness and neural activation in reward and emotion-regulation regions
experiment: Units are individuals receiving economic offers varying in fairness while payoff is controlled. Self-reported happiness is collected; neural responses to offers are measured via fMRI. Dependent variables include happiness ratings and regional brain activation (reward regions, insula, ventrolateral prefrontal cortex). Analysis compares activation patterns and happiness between fair and unfair equal-payoff offers. Secondary analysis examines whether individual differences in unfair offer acceptance relate to emotion-regulation activation and negative-affect suppression.

what_manipulated: attentional focus (items under focus vs items in visual periphery)
what_measured: precision of spatial localization and ensemble (statistical summary) feature representation
experiment: Units are individuals viewing arrays of visual objects while attention is directed toward some items and withheld from others. Dependent measures include accuracy of individual object localization and accuracy of centroid (center-of-mass) identification. Analysis compares localization precision between attended and unattended items, and between local (individual position) and summary (centroid) features. Statistical tests examine whether attention withdrawal degrades local detail more than statistical summary representation.

what_manipulated: implied motion direction in static photograph presentations (motion in one direction repeatedly presented vs no motion or motion in other direction)
what_measured: motion aftereffect strength (bias to perceive subsequent motion in opposite direction)
experiment: Units are individuals viewing series of static images conveying motion in a particular direction (e.g., action photographs), then tested with real-motion probes. Dependent variable is motion aftereffect magnitude, measured as the motion direction bias in test motion judgments. Analysis tests whether implied motion from photographs produces direction-selective adaptation equivalent to real motion adaptation. Comparisons examine transfer of adaptation from implied to real motion, indicating shared neural mechanisms.

what_manipulated: external motivation to avoid prejudice (measured individual difference) and stimulus presentation duration (30ms vs 450ms)
what_measured: attentional bias toward or away from racial outgroup members showing neutral versus happy expressions
experiment: Units are individuals classified as high or low in external motivation to avoid prejudice, then view pairs of White and Black faces with neutral or happy expressions displayed briefly (30ms) or longer (450ms). Dependent variable is attentional bias, measured via reaction time to probes replacing one face. Analysis compares bias patterns across motivation levels and presentation durations, testing whether external motivation creates vigilance to threat (neutral Black faces) at short duration and avoidance at long duration. Expression type is examined as moderator.
